story:
Like a camphor box closed with a cracked lid. That is how I feel. An amorphous lump at the core. Strangers fill the living room; my brother speaks for me; the CO’s wife offers kindness I cannot hold. I hold only our baby, three months old, swaddled like a bunny. “It’s time,” my brother says, eyes rimmed red, shoulders fallen. I should be brave for her.

It was not a bullet. It was a motor bike. One blow that flung me from the plains back to the hills. I had come down in your arms with our child in my womb. I return with our child in my arms, you in my spirit. Ma had warned me: men from the plains are trouble. Even in uniform. You did not look like trouble when my brother brought you home—classmates, coursemates, posted together in Daporijo. Your parents were gone; mine could not come. Thank God for their absence, because I broke anyway.

Home among bamboo-sheltered grounds, my ancestors lie; their spent energies feed our rice. I strap our girl to my jig-jiro, bend to the paddy, sing while water laughs in the channels. She drinks the milk of me and my sisters. She toddles the length of beaten bamboo floors, zigzag like the patterns Ma weaves. She runs soon after—with the strong lungs of my people and your long stride. The hills give, but they test takers. Too many from the plains never learn. I wonder what our daughter will learn.

My brother comes on leave, without his city wife; thin air cramps her. Our parents say both their children have wandered. They curse boarding school. I do not. Our girl plays at his knees; he looks in her eyes and cries. “She needs more than hills,” he says as Kile Pakho bleeds down. “She grows where I stay.” “Have you found no one?” “She is all I need.” No tears. My food comes from my dead; my life from you.

He takes us to his cantonment. He enrolls her in playschool and me in a baking class. His wife, when she tries, admires my weaves, sketches them into her designs, mouths Tani that makes him blush. Like you made me blush when you knelt and asked, “Will you make me a hungry man?”

Years whirl—tongues, failures, betrayals—and Dawn-Lit rises, my small patisserie with a name. Today our daughter comes home for winter break. I leave early; the assistants hand me a chocolate-orange stollen, her favorite. Popcorn hears the car before I do. She laughs as the dog leaps; you loved my laugh like that. I could not make it again after that day.

She devours stollen after bamboo rice and fish with tapyo. We watch Tom and Jerry; then she chooses her phone over albums. Her eyes glow; the smile crests. I fear she has met her trouble. She kisses me goodnight. I try the television, then pull an album anyway. The first photo stops me—Myko day, cheeks redder than my skirt, your eyes holding sky in my blue beads.

Pictures braid like a fresh cane basket: me leaving Ma and Pa to be with you; you crowning me when I carried our child; our small quarrels when she kept us awake; and the news on our first anniversary. You had to go to the hills at midnight—not my parents’ home, the border outpost. You cupped my face. “It will be all right. I will be back soon.” I was upset. I do not remember the kiss. I wish I did.
